Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, usually setting aside time in the evenings after my other responsibilities. I'd aim for about four hours a day spread across the week, mostly reading through materials and taking notes by hand. Honestly, it wasn't always exciting, but I knew I had to put in the hours to pass. Sometimes I'd lose focus because the content just didn't grab me, but I pushed through anyway.
Q2: My pattern was somewhat regular, but deadlines definitely shifted things around. I'd have my usual evening study blocks, but when an assignment was coming up, I'd cram more hours in the days before. With limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when I could access things online versus when I could work offline with what I'd already downloaded or written down.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check if something new was posted or when an assignment deadline was approaching. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post and that would prompt me to go in. But honestly, I didn't log in just to browse around—I went in with a specific purpose because my internet time is kind of limited and I needed to make it count.
Q4: There was a period mid-semester where I got into a rhythm of logging in maybe twice a week to check announcements and download any new readings. It became routine because I scheduled it around when I knew I'd have reliable internet access. But I wouldn't say it ever felt natural or automatic—it was more like a task I had to remember to do.
Q5: The reading materials and written lecture notes were the most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing things out. I'd download PDFs and then work through them on my own time, highlighting and making my own summaries. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful when other students explained concepts in different ways, though I didn't participate much myself.
Q6: I mostly ignored the video content—I watched some of it, but sitting through long videos felt tedious and used up a lot of my data. I also didn't engage much with the peer interaction features beyond what was required. It's not that I thought they were useless, but with my anxiety about participating and my limited resources, they just fell to the bottom of my priorities.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to the assignments or exams. If I read something and thought, 'okay, this will help me answer questions on the test,' then I'd engage with it fully. Anything that felt like busywork or extra fluff just made me feel bored and frustrated because I have limited time and energy to spare.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. I'd look at them and feel anxious because part of me thought I should do them, but then I'd weigh it against everything else I had to do and they'd get pushed aside. Required activities had a deadline attached, so they got done. Optional stuff just floated in this anxious limbo where I felt guilty for not doing it but also too drained to actually engage.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download the readings and then work through them on paper, making handwritten notes and summaries. I also used a textbook I borrowed from someone and some free resources I found online that I saved to my phone. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were many times where I'd spend hours working through my handwritten notes, re-reading passages, and writing out practice answers—none of which would show up anywhere in the system. I'd also study with a friend occasionally, going over concepts together, but since we weren't doing it through the platform, there's no record of it. My real study time was way more than what my login frequency suggests.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd log in, download everything I needed, and then log out and study offline for days. I couldn't afford to be online constantly, so I'd grab materials in batches and then work through them on my own schedule. One download could fuel a whole week of studying without me touching the LMS again.
Q12: There were definitely moments when my anxiety spiked and I just couldn't bring myself to engage with the course. Sometimes the workload felt overwhelming even though I knew I could handle it if I just started. I'd feel this heavy boredom mixed with dread, and I'd end up doing the bare minimum or avoiding things entirely until the pressure of a deadline forced me to act.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor—studying 27 hours a week while dealing with anxiety is exhausting. When I was tired, I'd log into the LMS and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities at home also meant I sometimes had to choose between logging in or dealing with real-life stuff, and the LMS usually lost that battle.
Q14: Oh, definitely. There were times I'd open a video or reading and my mind would just wander completely. I'd technically be 'watching' something but thinking about a hundred other things, feeling this flat boredom that made nothing stick. I'd reach the end and realize I'd have to go through it again because I hadn't actually learned anything the first time.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they made me anxious. The idea of putting my thoughts out there for others to see felt uncomfortable, so I'd wait until the last possible moment. I also avoided some assignments when I didn't fully understand what was being asked—instead of seeking help, I'd just delay and hope clarity would come, which it usually didn't.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine that drove my LMS use. Without a deadline, I'd drift and procrastinate, but once that date was approaching, I'd suddenly become very active—downloading materials, checking requirements, posting whatever was needed. My engagement was very spiky around due dates and pretty flat otherwise.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got positive feedback, it gave me a small boost, but it faded quickly. Negative or critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid looking at the course for a while afterward. I know feedback is supposed to help, but emotionally it often just added to my stress rather than motivating me.
Q18: The course structure was pretty straightforward, which helped because I knew what to expect each week. But the pacing sometimes felt relentless—by the time I finished one thing, another was already due. The structure gave me a framework, but it didn't really accommodate for students like me who need more time to process things or who work mostly offline.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement easier because I could study on my own terms. But the emphasis on video content and online discussions made things harder since those require consistent internet access and a level of social participation that triggers my anxiety. A more flexible approach to how we could demonstrate learning would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding but struggling to produce work that meets the requirements. They could be reading everything, watching videos, trying to figure things out, but then hitting a wall when it comes to actually completing assignments. Or maybe they're anxious about submitting imperfect work, so they keep preparing but never feel ready to turn something in.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. I studied a lot offline, so my LMS activity looks low even though I was putting in serious hours. I passed the course and did okay on assignments, but if you only looked at my login frequency, you'd think I barely tried. The system just can't see the work that happens away from the screen.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when people log in, what they click on, how long they spend on pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't measure understanding, anxiety, or effort. They see the shadow of learning, not the learning itself. For someone like me who works mostly offline, the logs tell maybe 30% of the story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually thinking about the material, wrestling with concepts, and trying to connect new information to what I already know. It's not about how many times I click on something—it's about whether my brain is actively working on understanding. For me, real engagement often happens quietly, alone, with a pen and paper, far from any platform that could track it.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety I carried throughout the course, or how hard I worked to manage it while still completing my work. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with handwritten notes, the times I explained concepts to myself out loud, or how I'd re-read the same passage five times until it clicked. It also wouldn't show how bored and disconnected I often felt, even when I was technically doing everything right.
